retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pancreatitis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The recurrence rate of acute pancreatitis depends on the underlying cause, ranging from 0.6% to 5.6%, with the highest rates observed in alcohol-related cases. [13] These figures underscore the importance of identifying and addressing the causative factor during the initial episode to prevent recurrence. Mortality in the US has declined despite rising hospitalization rates, likely due to earlier recognition, improved supportive care, and widespread use of standardized severity assessment tools such as the Revised Atlanta Classification and the Bedside Index for Severity in Acute Pancreatitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pancreatitis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of acute pancreatitis is increasing in the US and globally. Whether this rise reflects a true increase in disease burden or improved diagnostic recognition remains uncertain, but the trend is likely multifactorial. Contributing factors include rising rates of obesity, metabolic syndrome, and associated hypertriglyceridemia, all of which are recognized risk factors for pancreatitis. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pancreatitis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In the US, acute pancreatitis accounts for an estimated 200,000 to 275,000 hospital admissions annually, making it the leading cause of hospitalization for gastrointestinal disease. The condition is common, but clinical severity varies. Approximately 80% of patients present with mild disease and are discharged within several days. The overall mortality rate ranges from 1% to 2%, but increases substantially in cases involving pancreatic necrosis or systemic organ failure.

retrieved_docs.title: Alcoholic Pancreatitis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications from this disease can have both localized and systemic effects, including: Acute peri-pancreatic fluid collection, seen as a homogenous fluid collection adjacent to the pancreas on CT imaging, usually beginning <4 weeks after symptoms onset. Pancreatic pseudocyst, seen as a walled-off fluid collection, usually arising >4 weeks from symptom onset. Necrotizing pancreatitis (5% to 10%), seen as an intra/extra-pancreatic heterogeneous fluid collection and necrosis Walled-off necrosis, seen as a heterogeneous collection of fluid and necrosis with a well-defined wall, usually occurring >4 weeks from symptom onset. Systemic complications include sepsis, bacteremia from the migration of intestinal flora, pleural effusions, ARDS, and shock. Chronic pancreatitis patients are also at increased risk of developing pancreatic cancer, pancreatic diabetes, bile duct obstruction, and splenic vein thrombosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pancreatitis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The overall mortality of acute pancreatitis is approximately 1% to 2%, but this figure increases substantially in cases classified as severe, although reported rates vary across studies. Early assessment of disease severity is critical for determining the appropriate level of care, triage, intensive care unit admission, and timing of intervention. Numerous scoring systems have been developed to aid in risk stratification. Older tools, such as the Ranson criteria and the Acute Physiology and Chronic Health Evaluation (APACHE) II score, require data over the first 48 hours and may be impractical for routine use due to their complexity.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pancreatitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The Revised Atlanta Classification provides standardized terminology for the morphological subtypes and severity of acute pancreatitis (see Table . Morphologic Subtypes of Acute Pancreatitis Based on the Revised Atlanta Classification). Interstitial edematous pancreatitis involves inflammation of the pancreatic parenchyma and peripancreatic tissue without necrosis. This form is more common and generally less severe. In contrast, necrotizing pancreatitis includes varying degrees of pancreatic parenchymal or peripancreatic necrosis and is associated with a higher risk of complications and poorer clinical outcomes (see Image . Necrotizing Pancreatitis with Extensive Peripancreatic Nodularity).

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pancreatitis -- Etiology -- Biliary Pancreatitis
retrieved_docs.content: The risk of recurrence is high without definitive management. In patients with mild disease, cholecystectomy is recommended during the same hospitalization. Surgery may be deferred in individuals with moderate-to-severe disease until the acute inflammatory phase resolves.

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreas Imaging -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Pancreatitis is a generalized inflammation of the pancreas due to the activation of digestive enzymes produced by the pancreas while still inside the organ. Acutely, this can result from gallstones, alcohol misuse, or hypertriglyceridemia. [2] [3] Gallstones are the etiology of the majority of acute pancreatitis cases, 40% to 70%, while alcohol misuse accounts for 25% to 35%. [4] [5] Pancreatitis typically presents as an acute onset of epigastric pain that may radiate through to the back. Nausea and vomiting frequently accompany abdominal pain. The revised Atlanta classification separates acute pancreatitis into two subtypes: interstitial edematous pancreatitis and necrotizing pancreatitis. [6] The severity further classifies acute pancreatitis into mild, moderately severe, or severe, based on the absence of organ failure, the presence of transient organ failure, or persistent organ failure, respectively. [7] The mortality rate from acute pancreatitis is approximately 5%, while those with necrotizing pancreatitis see a higher rate at 17%. [8] Chronic pancreatitis can occur in patients with alcohol use disorder, in which scarring of the gland prevents it from functioning properly. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pancreatitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: These genetic alterations increase the risk of early-onset or recurrent acute pancreatitis and promote progression to chronic pancreatitis, a condition marked by irreversible fibrosis, acinar cell atrophy, and eventual endocrine and exocrine insufficiency. The pathogenesis of acute pancreatitis reflects a combination of premature enzyme activation, amplification of the inflammatory response, and systemic organ injury. Clinical outcomes depend on the intensity of the initial insult and the host’s genetic and physiologic capacity to contain inflammation and tissue damage.

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatitis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Acute pancreatitis accounts for about 275,000 hospital admissions annually. [2] Eighty percent of patients admitted with pancreatitis usually have mild disease and can be discharged within a few days. Overall mortality of acute pancreatitis is approximately 2%. [2] The relapse rate of acute pancreatitis is between 0.6% to 5.6%, and this depends on the etiology of pancreatitis. The relapse rate is highest when pancreatitis is due to alcohol use. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pancreatitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Gallstones and alcohol use account for approximately 35% to 40% and 17% to 25% of acute pancreatitis cases in the United States (US), respectively. [7] [8] Less common etiologies span mechanical, metabolic, infectious, autoimmune, toxic, genetic, and idiopathic mechanisms (see Image . Causes of Pancreatitis). Determining the underlying cause is critical for immediate management and recurrence prevention.

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatitis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of acute pancreatitis can be divided into local and systemic. Peripancreatic fluid collections usually develop in less than 4 weeks after the initial presentation of pancreatitis whereas a pseudocyst and walled-off necrosis more than 4 weeks after the onset of acute pancreatitis.Local complications based on the revised Atlanta criteria include the following: Pancreatic pseudocyst( > 4 weeks) Walled-off necrosis ( > 4 weeks) Peri-pancreatic fluid collection(early phase) Acute necrotic collection( < 4 weeks) Systemic complications include the following: Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) Compartment syndrome Acute kidney injury (AKI) Disseminated intravascular coagulation(DIC) Chronic pancreatitis has several complications including: Formation of pseudocysts Diabetes Pseudoaneurysms Splenic vein thrombosis Recurrent acute pancreatitis Risk of progression to pancreatic cancer

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatitis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Mortality from acute pancreatitis is about 2%. [2] After an episode of acute pancreatitis, endocrine and exocrine insufficiency can develop in about 20% to 30% of patients. [5] [10] [11] Risk factors for recurrent attacks include the etiology of the first attack, the severity of the initial attack, and the degree of pancreatic structural damage. There are several scoring systems that can stratify the severity of acute pancreatitis: Acute Physiology and Chronic Health Evaluation II (APACHE II), APACHE combined with scoring for obesity (APACHE-O), the Glasgow scoring system, the Harmless Acute Pancreatitis Score (HAPS), PANC 3, the Japanese Severity Score (JSS), Pancreatitis Outcome Prediction (POP), and the Bedside Index for Severity in Acute Pancreatitis (BISAP). [11] These scoring systems are not superior to frequent reassessment by clinicians and have been shown to have a high false-positive rate.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pancreatitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Severity is categorized into 3 clinical grades (see Table . Severity Staging of Acute Pancreatitis  Based on the Revised Atlanta Classification). Mild acute pancreatitis is defined by the absence of organ failure and local or systemic complications. Moderately severe disease involves transient organ failure lasting less than 48 hours or local or systemic complications such as peripancreatic fluid collections. Severe acute pancreatitis is marked by persistent organ failure exceeding 48 hours, often involving multiple organ systems. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Pancreatitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: A proposed explanation is that alcoholic pancreatitis is more likely to recur than biliary pancreatitis, which typically occurs only once. Continued alcohol consumption contributes to progressive pancreatic damage. In addition, each recurrent episode causes further injury to an already compromised pancreas. By the third episode and beyond, acute pancreatitis tends to be more severe than the initial occurrences. Individuals with 3 or more episodes of acute pancreatitis often exhibit clinical, biochemical, and imaging findings consistent with chronic pancreatitis. [18] A conceptual model outlining the progression of chronic pancreatitis has been published. [3] The model describes  the following distinct stages: At risk: Individuals are asymptomatic before the first pancreatic injury. Acute pancreatitis to recurrent acute pancreatitis: The first (sentinel) episode of acute pancreatitis occurs, followed by recurrent acute pancreatitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pancreatitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Acute pancreatitis is a common and potentially serious condition marked by abrupt inflammation of the pancreas. This condition is the leading cause of hospitalization for gastrointestinal disease in the United States, accounting for more than 275,000 hospital admissions annually. [1] The clinical course varies from mild, self-limited inflammation to severe disease with necrosis, systemic inflammatory response syndrome, multiorgan dysfunction syndrome, and high mortality. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2841
retrieved_docs.content: Unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ) are both clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). Hypercholesterolemia, defined by elevated blood cholesterol levels, is a recognized risk factor for atherosclerotic disease that can precipitate unstable angina, and it may also contribute to inflammatory conditions such as acute pancreatitis. Recognizing these connections highlights the importance of effective lipid management to reduce the risk of both cardiovascular events and pancreatic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2843
retrieved_docs.content: Acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ) is clinically associated with hypochloremia (نقص كلوريد الدم), meaning that patients experiencing an acute inflammatory attack of the pancreas often exhibit low serum chloride levels, which can reflect the fluid and electrolyte disturbances commonly seen in this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 0 -- Community 25
retrieved_docs.content: is contraindicated in pancreatitis, and that pancreatic disorders unrelated to diabetes are mapped to pancreatitis. Pancreatitis is associated with increased systemic and tissue alkalinity, a factor that may affect diagnostic interpretation and therapeutic choices.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1589
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم) is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 2). Acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ) is clinically associated both with hypercholesterolemia and with chloride deficiency (نقص كلوريد الدم). These relationships indicate that patients with type 2 diabetes often have elevated cholesterol levels, which can increase the risk of developing acute pancreatitis, and that the inflammatory episode of acute pancreatitis may be accompanied by electrolyte disturbances such as low serum chloride. Recognizing the links among type 2 diabetes, hypercholesterolemia, acute pancreatitis, and chloride deficiency supports more comprehensive evaluation and integrated management of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: Acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ) is clinically associated with elevated blood cholesterol levels (فرط كوليسترول الدم) as well as reduced blood chloride concentrations (نقص كلوريد الدم). These biochemical abnormalities can serve as important clues in the evaluation of patients with acute pancreatitis, influencing both diagnostic considerations and therapeutic strategies. Recognizing the link between hypercholesterolemia and hypochloremia in the context of acute pancreatic inflammation helps clinicians monitor metabolic disturbances, tailor supportive care, and potentially address underlying lipid abnormalities to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2829
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم) is clinically associated with unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة), acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ), and chest pain (ألم الصَدْر). These connections indicate that elevated cholesterol levels can contribute to cardiovascular manifestations such as unstable angina and chest pain, while also being linked to inflammatory conditions like acute pancreatitis. Recognizing these relationships supports a more comprehensive evaluation and management strategy for patients with high cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: فرط كوليسترول الدم (hypercholesterolemia) is clinically associated with مرض السكري نوع 2 (type 2 diabetes mellitus), indicating that elevated blood cholesterol frequently co‑occurs with this form of diabetes. In addition, الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ (acute pancreatitis) is clinically associated with فرط كوليسترول الدم (hypercholesterolemia), suggesting that high cholesterol levels may also be linked to the development of severe pancreatic inflammation. Together, these connections imply that hypercholesterolemia can serve as a common clinical link between type 2 diabetes and acute pancreatitis, underscoring the importance of lipid monitoring and management in diabetic patients to potentially mitigate the risk of pancreatitis and its complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1860
retrieved_docs.content: Plasma hyperosmolarity (ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما) is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose (ارتفاع السكر في الدم) and is a common manifestation in both type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1) and type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 2). It is also linked to insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين) and can coexist with low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم). Dehydration (التجفاف) is mapped to plasma hyperosmolarity, indicating that fluid loss often precipitates the hyperosmolar state, and dehydration itself is clinically associated with acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد). Acute kidney failure is further clinically associated with type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes, acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ), dehydration, abnormal mental states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية), and low blood volume. These interconnections highlight that disturbances in fluid balance and severe hyperglycemia can lead to plasma hyperosmolarity, which together with diabetes‑related metabolic stress may precipitate acute kidney failure, often accompanied by hypovolemia, mental status changes, and pancreatic inflammation. Recognizing these linked clinical features is essential for timely diagnosis and comprehensive management of patients presenting with hyperosmolar, diabetic, or renal complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 1791
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are described as closely related and possibly synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. Within this category, abdominal pain (ألم بطني) is identified as a specific child manifestation. Abdominal pain is also clinically associated with pancreatic disorders that are not diabetes, indicating that non‑diabetic pancreatic disease often presents with this symptom. Recognizing that abdominal pain belongs to the ill‑defined signs and symptoms group and may signal underlying pancreatic pathology helps clinicians prioritize appropriate diagnostic evaluation and management for patients presenting with unexplained abdominal discomfort.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2860
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with several important medical conditions. It is linked to type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1), indicating that patients with this form of diabetes may be at increased risk for developing acute kidney failure and that renal impairment can complicate diabetes management. Acute kidney failure is also associated with acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ), reflecting a bidirectional relationship in which severe pancreatic inflammation can precipitate renal injury and renal dysfunction can exacerbate pancreatic disease. In addition, an abnormal psychological state (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية) is clinically associated with acute kidney failure, suggesting that mental health disturbances may coexist with, contribute to, or result from severe renal impairment. Finally, acute kidney failure is associated with insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين), underscoring the heightened vulnerability of patients with advanced type 2 diabetes to renal complications. Recognizing these interconnections is essential for comprehensive patient assessment, early detection of comorbidities, and coordinated management of both renal and metabolic or psychological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2837
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal pain (ألم بطني) is frequently observed alongside nausea and vomiting, indicating a clinical association between these gastrointestinal symptoms. In addition, pancreatic disorders that are not diabetes also commonly present with abdominal pain, highlighting the relevance of abdominal pain as a key sign in evaluating pancreatic disease. Recognizing that abdominal pain can co‑occur with both nausea/vomiting and non‑diabetic pancreatic pathology aids clinicians in differential diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1287
retrieved_docs.content: Pancreatic disorders (not diabetes) are clinically associated with زيادة قلوية الدم والأنسجة (increased alkalinity of the blood and tissues) and are mapped to الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياس (pancreatitis), an inflammatory condition of the pancreas. This connection indicates that non‑diabetic pancreatic disease often presents with systemic alkalosis and corresponds to pancreatitis, emphasizing the need to monitor blood pH and recognize pancreatic inflammation in patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2145
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم) is clinically associated with both type 1 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 1) and type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 2), indicating that elevated blood cholesterol frequently co‑occurs with these distinct forms of diabetes. In addition, hypercholesterolemia is clinically associated with acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ), suggesting that patients with markedly high cholesterol levels may have an increased risk of developing inflammatory pancreatic disease. Acute pancreatitis itself is clinically associated with hypochloremia (نقص كلوريد الدم), reflecting that severe pancreatic inflammation can lead to disturbances in electrolyte balance, particularly low blood chloride. Together, these relationships highlight a network of metabolic and inflammatory connections: dyslipidemia links diabetes and pancreatic injury, while pancreatic inflammation can further disrupt electrolyte homeostasis, underscoring the importance of comprehensive monitoring and integrated management of lipid levels, glycemic control, pancreatic health, and electrolyte status in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 886
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related and possibly synonymous with signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. This broader category functions as a parent concept that includes specific clinical manifestations such as ألم بطني (abdominal pain) and الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse). Abdominal pain is clinically associated with pancreatic disorders (not diabetes), indicating that non‑diabetic pancreatic disease often presents with this symptom. Additionally, diseases and injuries are organized under the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting that many pathological entities are expressed through such nonspecific signs. Recognizing that vague or ill‑defined symptom clusters—including abdominal pain and circulatory collapse—may signal underlying pancreatic pathology or other disease processes helps clinicians adopt a systematic approach to assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Pancreatic disorders (not diabetes) are clinically associated with ألم بطني (abdominal pain), indicating that patients with non‑diabetic pancreatic conditions often present with abdominal pain as a key clinical feature.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1861
retrieved_docs.content: Fluid and electrolyte disorders serve as a broad classification for cases of fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base imbalance (حالات اضطراب توازن السائل والإلكتروليت والقاعدي الحمضي). These disorders are closely linked to diabetic hyperosmolar coma (غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري), reflecting the critical role of osmotic and electrolyte disturbances in this acute metabolic emergency. Clinically, alcoholic hepatitis (الْتِهابُ الكَبِدِ الكحوليّ) is frequently associated with such fluid‑electrolyte‑acid‑base disturbances, underscoring the need for careful monitoring of volume status and electrolyte balance in affected patients. Similarly, alcoholic pancreatitis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is commonly associated with hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم), highlighting a pattern of potassium excess that may accompany pancreatic inflammation in the context of alcohol use. Acid‑base imbalance (اختلال التوازن الحمضي القاعدي) is a parent category that encompasses the specific cases of fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base disturbance. Within this spectrum, acidosis (الحماض) is identified as a distinct type of imbalance and often co‑occurs with hyperkalemia, indicating that low pH states frequently accompany elevated serum potassium levels. Respiratory alkalosis (تَقَلْوِن تنفسي) represents a particular subtype of acid‑base imbalance, illustrating how respiratory mechanisms can produce a primary shift toward alkalinity within the broader context of acid‑base disorders. Together, these interrelationships emphasize the importance of integrated assessment of fluid status, electrolyte concentrations, and acid‑base balance in patients with metabolic, hepatic, and pancreatic conditions, especially when alcohol‑related pathology is present.
